scapegoat.name: celestial causes
scapegoat.justification: The text attributes human actions to three celestial causes—God, an Angel, and a celestial body—which deconstructs human agency by framing will, intellect, and bodily action as governed externally rather than internally.

moral_justification: Human actions are not self-determined but subject to divine and celestial control; good works are willed by God, rendering human choice subordinate and eliminating genuine moral autonomy.
summary: The passage deconstructs human agency by asserting that will, intellect, and bodily action are governed by external celestial forces—God, an Angel, and a celestial body—citing scriptural authority to show divine sovereignty over human choices, thus undermining self-governance and moral responsibility.
